people.name: Constantine the Great
people.summary: Brownworth identifies Constantine as the conventional starting point of the Byzantine Empire, crediting him with refounding the city of Byzantium as Constantinople to serve as a new Christian imperial capital. He notes that Constantine himself was undeniably Roman, standing in an unbroken line of emperors from Augustus, which makes labeling him the founder of a 'Byzantine' empire complicated.

people.name: Constantine Palaiologos
people.summary: Brownworth marks Constantine Palaiologos as the end point of Byzantine history, describing him dying in the streets of Constantinople fighting the Turks in 1453. He notes that Palaiologos was the 137th emperor in an unbroken line stretching all the way back to Augustus Caesar.

people.name: Diocletian
people.summary: Brownworth names Diocletian as his chosen starting point for Byzantine history, describing him as the brilliant, ambitious emperor who split the empire into eastern and western halves in 285 AD and then, remarkably, walked away from power. He is placed in the 'troubled third century' and will be the subject of the next episode.

people.name: Augustus Caesar
people.summary: Brownworth cites Augustus as the first Roman Emperor and the anchor of the unbroken imperial line, to which both Constantine the Great and the last emperor Constantine Palaiologos belong. He also uses Augustus' reign as an example of how dramatically Roman society could change within a single generation, with Romans accepting emperorship after centuries of abhorring tyrants.

people.name: Edward Gibbon
people.role: Historian, author of The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire
people.summary: Brownworth describes Gibbon as the first modern historian, whose 1776 masterwork 'The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire' shaped — and largely poisoned — how the Byzantine Empire was perceived for centuries. Gibbon's personal disillusionment with Christianity, stemming from a youthful conversion to Catholicism that his family forced him to renounce, led him to view Byzantium as 'a degenerate mongrel of Greece and Rome' and nothing more than a thousand years of decline and decay.

people.name: John Kantakouzenos
people.summary: Brownworth uses John Kantakouzenos as a vivid illustration of how far the Byzantine Empire had drifted from classical Roman identity by its final centuries: visiting Europe calling himself a Roman Emperor while dressed in turban-like headgear, slippers, attended by eunuchs, and performing 'effeminate Eastern ceremonies.' This image, Brownworth argues, made later observers judge the empire as something wholly un-Roman.

people.name: Robert F. Kennedy
people.summary: Brownworth quotes Kennedy's observation that 'few will have the greatness to bend history itself,' using it to argue that great individuals — not masses or movements — are the true engines of history, and thus the right lens through which to tell Byzantine history.

people.name: Alexander the Great
people.summary: Brownworth mentions Alexander the Great as an example of a towering individual whose story is essential for understanding history, arguing it matters far more than knowing what a Macedonian peasant thought of him.

people.name: Martin Luther
people.role: Theologian, leader of the Protestant Reformation
people.summary: Brownworth invokes Luther as an example proving that great historical movements — in this case the Reformation — are incomprehensible without understanding the key individuals who drove them.

people.name: Ulrich Zwingli
people.summary: Mentioned alongside Luther and Calvin as key individuals of the Reformation whose stories are essential to understanding that historical movement.

people.name: John Calvin
people.summary: Mentioned alongside Luther and Zwingli as key individuals of the Reformation whose stories are essential to understanding that historical movement.

people.name: George Washington
people.role: General, first President of the United States
people.summary: Named by Brownworth as one of the key individuals without whom the American Revolution cannot be understood, used to illustrate his argument that individuals drive history more than masses or movements.

people.name: Alexander Hamilton
people.role: Founding Father of the United States
people.summary: Named by Brownworth alongside Washington and Franklin as a key individual essential to understanding the American Revolution.

people.name: Benjamin Franklin
people.role: Founding Father of the United States
people.summary: Named by Brownworth alongside Washington and Hamilton as a key individual essential to understanding the American Revolution.

people.name: King George III
people.summary: Named by Brownworth as one of the key individuals — alongside Washington, Hamilton, Franklin, and Pitt — without whom the American Revolution cannot be properly understood.

people.name: William Pitt
people.summary: Named by Brownworth alongside Washington, Hamilton, Franklin, and King George as a key individual essential to understanding the American Revolution.

people.name: Thomas Jefferson
people.role: Founding Father, author of the Declaration of Independence
people.summary: Brownworth mentions Jefferson only in passing to anchor Edward Gibbon's publication date: the first volume of 'The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire' appeared in 1776, the same year Jefferson penned the Declaration of Independence.

people.name: William Shakespeare
people.summary: Brownworth uses Shakespeare as an analogy for how language and society change over time: even after only 400 years, Shakespeare's English is nearly incomprehensible to modern high schoolers, making it all the more understandable that the Byzantine Empire would look unrecognizable after a thousand years.

people.name: Brutus
people.role: Roman senator and assassin of Julius Caesar
people.summary: Brownworth invokes Brutus and the early Roman founding fathers as representatives of a Rome that fiercely abhorred tyrants, contrasting that with the Rome of Augustus' time that willingly sought an emperor — illustrating how dramatically a civilization can change within a generation.

places.name: Constantinople
places.summary: Constantinople is the central place of Byzantine history as Brownworth frames it: founded by Constantine the Great on the site of old Byzantium, it served as the Christian imperial capital meant to rival or surpass Rome. The city's founding in 324–330 is one of the three candidate start-dates Brownworth considers for Byzantine history, and it is the city where the last emperor Constantine Palaiologos died fighting the Turks in 1453.

places.name: Byzantium
places.summary: Byzantium is the ancient Greek city on the Bosphorus that Constantine refounded as Constantinople. Brownworth notes that later historians reached back to this original name — rather than the unwieldy 'Constantinopolitan Empire' — to coin the term 'Byzantine Empire.'

places.name: Rome
places.summary: Rome is the original imperial capital and the yardstick against which Byzantine identity is measured throughout this episode. Brownworth notes that the empire later called Byzantine never controlled Rome for most of its history, which is precisely why historians felt the need to give it a different name; he also discusses how Rome itself changed enormously between the age of Brutus and the age of Augustus.

places.name: Bosphorus
places.summary: Brownworth mentions the Bosphorus as the geographic location on which Constantine began building his new capital in 324, making it the physical setting for the birth of the Byzantine world.

places.name: Macedonia
places.summary: Mentioned by Brownworth only as the homeland of a hypothetical peasant who might have had an opinion about Alexander the Great, used to illustrate the contrast between a common person's perspective and the significance of great individuals.

events.name: Founding of Constantinople
events.summary: Brownworth identifies the founding of Constantinople — Constantine refounding Byzantium as his new Christian imperial capital — as the conventional starting point most historians give to Byzantine history. He distinguishes two related dates: 324, when Constantine began building, and 330, when the city was formally dedicated.

events.name: Fall of Constantinople
events.summary: Brownworth marks 1453 as the end of Byzantine history, with Emperor Constantine Palaiologos dying in the streets of Constantinople fighting the Turks. This event ends the 137-emperor unbroken line stretching from Augustus.

events.name: Division of the Roman Empire
events.summary: Brownworth describes Diocletian splitting the Roman Empire into eastern and western halves in 285 AD, with Constantinople serving as capital of the eastern half. He lists this as one of three candidate starting points for Byzantine history and ultimately chooses it as his own preferred beginning.

events.name: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
events.summary: Brownworth notes that the Western Empire fell in 476, an event most people were taught marks 'the fall of Rome.' He argues this has led to a false perception that the Middle East jumped straight from a classical past to a Muslim present, ignoring the millennium of Byzantine history that continued in the East.

events.name: Publication of The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire (Vol. 1)
events.summary: Brownworth identifies Gibbon's 1776 publication as the watershed moment that established the dominant — and deeply negative — scholarly view of the Byzantine Empire. Published the same year as the Declaration of Independence, the work is still considered a must-read despite being more than 200 years old, and its anti-Byzantine bias created what Brownworth calls 'an almost a conspiracy of silence' around Byzantine history.

events.name: Protestant Reformation
events.summary: Brownworth invokes the Reformation as an example of a vast historical movement that is incomprehensible without studying the key individuals — Luther, Zwingli, and Calvin — who drove it, using it to justify his individual-centered approach to Byzantine history.

events.name: American Revolution
events.summary: Brownworth cites the American Revolution as another example of a historical movement whose full significance requires knowing its key individuals — Washington, Hamilton, Franklin, King George, and Pitt — rather than viewing it through broad strokes or the lens of the common person.

events.name: The Crusades
events.summary: Brownworth mentions the Crusades only in passing, noting that Western disregard for the Byzantine Empire predates even the Crusades and is made up of 'equal parts envy and disgust.' The Crusades thus represent a long-standing cultural rupture between Western and Eastern Christendom.
